title: Reading and Use of English – Part 1
description: Read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap.

content.text:
The way humans have developed their cuisine is a fascinating subject of study. Early humans (0) their diet mainly on what was available in their immediate surroundings. Over time, as they learned to cultivate plants and domesticate animals, their diets (1) to include a wider variety of foods.

The advent of agriculture can be (2) as one of the greatest innovations in human history. With the ability to grow their own food, societies began to (3) up cities and complex civilizations. This agricultural transformation was not (4) to chance; it required knowledge, skills, and the willingness to adapt to new environments.

As trade routes expanded, people (5) to exotic spices and ingredients from different parts of the world. This (6) to the development of unique regional cuisines. Indeed, many of the flavors we (7) today are the result of centuries of cultural exchange and influence.

Modern times have seen a move towards globalized diets, with fast food and processed meals becoming the (8) choice for many. This shift raises questions about the future of traditional cuisines and food sustainability.

example.explanation: The correct phrase is 'based their diet on', as 'base on' is a fixed usage.

questions.explanation: 'Expanded' fits because it's collocated with 'diets' to mean increasing variety. 'Widened' is a plausible distractor but refers more to scope rather than variety.
'Seen as' is a fixed phrase meaning considered or perceived. The other options don't use the appropriate preposition.
'Set up' is a phrasal verb meaning to establish. 'Built' suggests physical construction more than establishment of cities and civilizations.
'Restricted to' is the correct collocation as it implies limitation in scope due to the need for specific conditions.
'Exposed to' is the right collocation meaning to experience or come into contact with something.
'Led to' is an idiom meaning to cause something to happen. The other options don't fit the causation context properly.
'Enjoy' is the correct word as it usually collocates with the experience of consuming food. The others don't fit idiomatically with 'flavors'.
'Main choice' is a collocation meaning the principal selection. 'Prime choice' is a plausible distractor but conveys more a sense of quality than popularity.
